student_message: I know! It is a line of symmetry!
final_message:
You got it! It absolutely is a line of symmetry! Look right here with me for a second. 🦋 Did you know a butterfly’s wings have perfect reflection symmetry? If you fold a picture of one right down the middle, the patterns on both wings match up exactly! Can you think of any other animals that have a line of symmetry? 🤔 

While you think about that, let's try a fun puzzle using that same folding idea. Imagine Prem wants to cut out the letter V for a birthday banner using the fold-and-cut method. How many lines of symmetry does the letter V have? Tell me which direction the line goes, and how you think Prem should fold his paper!

orchestrator_intent.goal: Validate her realization and present a quiz to check understanding while regaining visual focus.
orchestrator_intent.reasoning: The student correctly recalled the math concept but has been visually distracted for 6 turns. Advancing to a quiz will test her knowledge, while the engagement agent will prompt her to look back at the screen.

current_section_title: Reflection Symmetry and Lines of Symmetry
current_section_content: A line of symmetry is a fold line that divides a shape into two halves that are perfect mirror images of each other. Some shapes have a vertical line of symmetry (left-right mirror), some have a horizontal line (top-bottom mirror), and some have both. You can use this idea to cut out shapes by drawing only half or one-quarter of the shape on folded paper, then cutting and unfolding. Letters like A have one vertical line of symmetry, while letters like H have both vertical and horizontal lines of symmetry.
session_summary: The session covered the concept of symmetry and lines of symmetry. The student understood the material well, successfully identifying a vertical line of symmetry on their own body. However, the student struggled significantly with focus and attention, frequently becoming distracted by off-topic subjects like their dog and favorite color. The student's current learning state reflects a solid grasp of basic symmetry, though they require constant redirection from the tutor to stay on task.
